events.summary: STORY: Samsung reported record quarterly profit on Thursday driven by a 49-fold jump in chip income.The South Korean tech giant said January-March operating profit in its chip division reached a record $36.15 billion.It was massively up from a year before and represented 94% of the quarter's total.Overall revenue rose 69% on the year to $90.4 billion.It marks a positive step for the firm which wants to close the gap with rival SK Hynix in supplying high bandwidth memory chips to Nvidia.SK Hynix last week reported record quarterly profit following a fivefold jump in earnings.The chipmakers have seen a boom in the construction of AI data centers.It's led firms to allocate production capacity to advanced chips Nvidia uses in its so-called AI accelerators.Chipmakers have struggled to meet demand while the move also squeezes the supply of conventional semiconductors.Samsung said Thursday - quote - its supply falls far short of customer demand.And it believes the supply-to-demand gap for 2027 is due to widen even further than this year.The firm said continued development in AI tech will lead to sustained demand growth.But supply will stay constrained for the time being due to the lead time required for new factory construction.Elsewhere, Samsung said conflict in the Middle East hasn't disrupted chipmaking.It cited secured inventory and diversified sources of gases used in manufacturing.But it warned there's a risk of higher transportation costs caused by rising oil prices.Samsung also faces possible production disruption as unions representing the majority of its workers in South Korea could strike over pay.Samsung said it "plans to respond to the fullest extent to ensure production isn't disrupted."Shares of the firm slipped almost 2.5% Thursday after rising as much as 1.8% following the earnings announcement.The stock has surged 88% this year.
events.title: Samsung chip profit jumps almost 50-fold; supply shortage to worsen

events.summary: STORY: U.S. stocks advanced on Thursday as solid corporate earnings offset the war-related oil supply shock that sent crude prices to four-year highs.The Dow added 1.6% while the S&P 500 rose 1% and the Nasdaq climbed a little less than that on the last trading day of April.The S&P had its best month in more than five years and the Nasdaq its best in six years.Economic data showed the U.S. economy continues to grow at a healthy pace as investors navigate geopolitical tensions.While inflation jumped to more than 3% on an annual basis from the rise in fuel costs as measured by the personal consumption expenditures price index,That is significantly higher than the Federal Reserve’s 2% target.However, Mike Bailey, director of research at FBB Capital Partners isn’t concerned. “We're looking at inflation heating up a little bit, but not to the level where investors are getting worried. So I think that's a plus. GDP is growing. You know, as you can kind of look across the board and everything seems a little mixed. So GDP is growing better than what we saw in the fourth quarter. Not quite as much growth as the investors wanted, but I think good enough.”Several members of the Magnificent Seven group of artificial intelligence-related megacaps reported late Wednesday.Alphabet jumped 10% after reporting a record quarter for its cloud unit.Meta fared much worse falling 8.5% and Microsoft slid 4% on concerns over artificial intelligence-related expenditures.Late Thursday Apple reported results that beat Wall Street estimates, while supply constraints hindered iPhone sales. The stock was lower in after-hours trading.:: Eli LillyOther stocks on the move included Eli Lilly, which gained nearly 10% after the drugmaker raised its annual profit forecast on sustained weight-loss drug demand.And Caterpillar also added 10% and hit an all-time high during the session after posting an increase in quarterly profit due to strong demand for its power generation and construction equipment.
